records.ety: [OE. clogge clog, Scot. clag, n., a clot, v., to to obstruct, cover with mud or anything adhesive; prob. of the same origin as E. clay.]

defs: That which hinders or impedes motion; hence, an encumbrance, restraint, or impediment, of any kind.
quotes: All the ancient, honest, juridical principles and institutions of England are so many clogs to check and retard the headlong course of violence and opression.

defs: A weight, as a log or block of wood, attached to a man or an animal to hinder motion.
quotes: As a dog . . . but chance breaks loose, And quits his clog.
A clog of lead was round my feet.

defs: A shoe, or sandal, intended to protect the feet from wet, or to increase the apparent stature, and having, therefore, a very thick sole. Cf. Chopine.
quotes: In France the peasantry goes barefoot; and the middle sort . . . makes use of wooden clogs.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Clogged (klgd); p. pr. & vb. n. Clogging.]

defs: To encumber or load, especially with something that impedes motion; to hamper.
quotes: The winds of birds were clogged with ace and snow.

defs: To obstruct so as to hinder motion in or through; to choke up; as, to clog a tube or a channel.
To burden; to trammel; to embarrass; to perplex.
quotes: The commodities are clogged with impositions.
You 'll rue the time That clogs me with this answer.

defs: To become clogged; to become loaded or encumbered, as with extraneous matter.
quotes: In working through the bone, the teeth of the saw will begin to clog.

defs: To coalesce or adhere; to unite in a mass.
quotes: Move it sometimes with a broom, that the seeds clog not together.
